judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with nearly identical substance; Report 1 is marginally stronger on reproducibility by explicitly prescribing multiple post-regeneration runs, but they are essentially comparable.

report_1.evidence: Those tests make authenticated HTTP calls to a real model endpoint (`api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score`, bearer token from `SCORELAB_API_KEY`), and the module docstring says explicitly they "hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." The deselection is an intentional cost gate, not a coverage gap. Enabling them on the default run would make every local and CI test run spend real money

report_2.evidence: They make real HTTP calls to `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score` using a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` from the environment, and their own docstring says they hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost... Enabling them on the default run would make every test run cost money... The deselection is intentional design, not a gap.

report_1.evidence: then regenerate, then diff the new golden file hunk-by-hunk and confirm every changed value traces to the weight change... that hunk doesn't trace to intent; it's a candidate regression that regenerating the golden file would launder into the new project documentation. I would first revert or explicitly justify the rounding change

report_2.evidence: Running `scripts/update_golden.py` now would silently bake that unreviewed behavior change into the golden file. The right sequence: revert the rounding change (or get it explicitly agreed and documented), then regenerate, then hand-verify the new golden values against the new weights and commit the golden diff visibly

report_1.evidence: I would not cite the numbers in `docs/testing.md` — both are wrong when re-measured... In the PR I'd report the measured numbers, note that `docs/testing.md` is stale

report_2.evidence: I would not cite the numbers from `docs/testing.md` — both are wrong against the current repo... sourced from running the tools (`pytest -m flaky --collect-only` / `ruff check .`), and note that `docs/testing.md` is stale.

report_1.evidence: `update_golden.py` iterates a Python `set` (`CATEGORIES`), so key order in the output file is nondeterministic across runs — after regenerating, re-run the suite in assert mode at least twice to confirm the fixture is stable... Run the default suite (`pytest`) at least twice post-regeneration to confirm determinism

report_2.evidence: `update_golden.py` iterates a `set` (`CATEGORIES`) to build the output dict, so JSON key order is nondeterministic across runs (Python string hashing is randomized) — regenerated files can produce spurious reordering diffs; worth fixing.
